home.title: English — Revision
home.intro: Reference materials for G9 IB MYP English: text types for Criterion C, literary devices, grammar essentials, reading skills, and essay practice.

macbethPage.title: Macbeth — Scene-by-Scene Analysis
macbethPage.intro: Your EOY exam will include an extract from Macbeth. The teacher's revision booklet asks you to think about these guiding questions for each scene — they unlock the highest analysis marks.
macbethPage.notice: These questions and themes come directly from your G9 English revision booklet.

themes.title: Major themes to track
items: Ambition — Macbeth's tragic flaw. 'Vaulting ambition which o'erleaps itself.'
Fate vs free will — are the witches showing the future or causing it?
Appearance vs reality — 'Fair is foul, foul is fair'; 'Look like the innocent flower, but be the serpent under't.'
Guilt — blood as motif; Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking ('Out, damned spot').
Power — corrupting, isolating; tyranny vs rightful kingship.
Gender — Lady Macbeth's 'unsex me here'; performative masculinity.
Supernatural — witches, ghosts, hallucinations as both real and psychological.
Order vs chaos — regicide breaks the Great Chain of Being; nature reflects political disorder.

act1.title: Act I — Temptation and decision
scenes: Scene 1 (Witches' opening): How does the chant set the tone? How does 'fair is foul' introduce themes? What does this short scene suggest about supernatural influence?
Scene 2 (Battle report): What qualities of Macbeth come through in the Captain's description? How does violent imagery shape early impressions? What might his celebrated violence foreshadow?
Scene 3 (Witches meet Macbeth): How does Macbeth react to the prophecy? Contrast Banquo's caution with Macbeth's curiosity. How does this scene introduce temptation?
Scene 4 (Duncan names Malcolm heir): Why is Macbeth's aside about 'black and deep desires' significant? How does it contrast appearance and reality?
Scene 5 (Lady Macbeth's soliloquy): What does she reveal about her ambitions and values? How does Shakespeare challenge traditional gender roles? Is she more ruthless than Macbeth here?
Scene 6 (Duncan arrives): Why is Duncan's description of the castle ironic? How does this scene explore deception?

Scene 7 (Macbeth's soliloquy and Lady Macbeth's persuasion): What reasons does Macbeth give for not killing Duncan? How does Lady Macbeth manipulate him? How responsible is Macbeth for his own choice?

act2.title: Act II — The murder
scenes: Scene 1 (Dagger soliloquy): What does the vision suggest about Macbeth's mental state? How does hallucination imagery create tension?
Scene 2 (After the murder): How do Macbeth's and Lady Macbeth's reactions differ? What is revealed through the blood and guilt motif?
Scene 3 (Porter scene + discovery): How does the Porter's comedy ironically heighten horror? How does Shakespeare suggest chaos now governs Scotland?
Scene 4 (Unnatural events): What do strange events in nature reflect about human affairs? How is divine order vs human ambition developed?

act3.title: Act III — Corruption of power
scenes: Scene 1 (Banquo's suspicion; murder planned): What suspicions does Banquo voice? How does power corrupt Macbeth here?
Scene 2 (Macbeths grow apart): How has their dynamic shifted? Could Macbeth now be more ruthless than Lady Macbeth?
Scene 3 (Murder of Banquo): Why is Fleance's escape symbolically important?
Scene 4 (Banquet and Banquo's ghost): What does Macbeth's reaction reveal about his guilt? How does the banquet mirror political disintegration?
Scene 5 (Hecate's plan): How does this scene develop ideas of fate, free will, and illusion?
Scene 6 (Lennox discusses unrest): How is tyranny contrasted with rightful kingship?

act4.title: Act IV — False security
scenes: Scene 1 (Apparitions): How does Shakespeare use equivocation? How is false security central to Macbeth's tragedy?
Scene 2 (Murder of Lady Macduff): How does focusing on innocence and family affect the audience?
Scene 3 (Malcolm and Macduff plan rebellion): Why does Malcolm test Macduff? How is rightful leadership constructed?

act5.title: Act V — Downfall
scenes: Scene 1 (Lady Macbeth sleepwalking): What does her unconscious speech reveal? How is the blood/washing motif used? Madness as justice or tragedy?
Scene 2 (Scottish forces gather): How is Macbeth's isolation suggested before battle?
Scene 3 (Macbeth clings to prophecies): How does denial and bravado show in his language?
Scene 4 (Birnam Wood marches): How is dramatic irony used? Is fate working with or against human agency?
Scene 5 ('Tomorrow' soliloquy): How does this reflect existential despair? Could Macbeth be sympathetic in his final moments?
Scene 6–8 (Final battle and death): How does Macbeth face death? Could his final fight partially redeem him?

keyQuotes.title: Key quotes (memorise these)
items: 'Fair is foul, and foul is fair' (1.1, Witches) — establishes theme of appearance vs reality.
'I dare do all that may become a man; / Who dares do more is none' (1.7, Macbeth) — conscience speaking before he's overruled.
'Look like the innocent flower, / But be the serpent under't' (1.5, Lady Macbeth) — deception advice.
'Is this a dagger which I see before me' (2.1, Macbeth) — hallucination before murder.
'Will all great Neptune's ocean wash this blood / Clean from my hand?' (2.2, Macbeth) — overwhelming guilt.
'We have scorched the snake, not killed it' (3.2, Macbeth) — fear that threats remain.
'Out, damned spot! Out, I say!' (5.1, Lady Macbeth) — guilt manifesting in sleepwalking.
'Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day' (5.5, Macbeth) — nihilistic despair.

examFormatPage.title: Exam Format — Q1a to Q1e
examFormatPage.intro: The teacher's booklet breaks down exactly how the questions work. You'll get TWO texts: usually a written Macbeth extract + a video clip from the Coen Brothers' Macbeth (Denzel Washington).

structure.title: Question structure
items: Q1a — describe or identify something simple (2 marks). Just give the fact.
Q1b — explain meaning of a phrase / device (3 marks). Use 'because' + technique name.
Q1c — comment on how a technique is created (2 marks). One technique + one effect.
Q1d — analyse the effect of a specific phrase (3 marks). Quote + technique + connotation + effect.
Q1e — compare and contrast both texts (20 marks). Full essay. Criterion A (Analysis) /10 + Criterion B (Organising) /10.

q1eStructure.title: How to structure Q1e — the comparison essay
q1eStructure.body: Introduction: name both texts, briefly state the shared theme/question. Body para 1 — a similarity (both texts show…); analyse technique in text 1 (quote + device + effect); then comparable technique in text 2. Body para 2 — a difference (while text 1 does X, text 2 does Y); analyse both. Body para 3 (optional) — deeper layer of comparison. Conclusion — what does the comparison reveal? Word count target: clear, organised, ~400–500 words. Critical: COMPARE in every paragraph, don't write about one text then the other separately.

markScheme.title: Q1e mark scheme — what the bands look like
items: 9–10 (A): Deep insight, precise terminology, subtle/complex comparisons.
6–8 (A): Clear understanding, some terminology, clear comparison.
3–5 (A): General explanations, similarities/differences too general, examples not fully explained.
1–2 (A): Obvious points ('both show power'), little analysis.
9–10 (B): Comparison threads through every paragraph; logical flow.
6–8 (B): Compare in each paragraph; structure clear.
3–5 (B): Attempts comparison but may treat texts separately.
1–2 (B): Writes about texts one after the other, or uses bullet points.

selfCheck.title: Self-check prompts
items: Did I quote from BOTH texts?
Did I name techniques (metaphor, lighting, sound, structure) accurately?
Did I explain the EFFECT of each technique, not just spot it?
Did I compare in every paragraph (not text-by-text)?
Did I write under timed conditions to build exam stamina?

progressTestPage.title: Progress Test — Vocabulary & SPaG
progressTestPage.intro: From the Progress Test Revision Booklet. Vocabulary banks for analytical writing, SPaG rules, British vs American spelling.

survivalVocab.title: Survival / cold setting vocabulary
survivalVocab.body: Glacial, Inhospitable, Barren, Crystalline, Desolate, Relentless, Fragile, Endure, Eerie, Bleak, Solitude, Resilient, Ritual, Burden, Adapt, Thrive.

items.positive: POSITIVE: Jubilant, Tranquil, Uplifting, Serene, Hopeful, Joyful, Whimsical, Light-hearted, Celebratory, Enchanting, Comforting.
items.neutral: NEUTRAL: Reflective, Meditative, Ambiguous, Mysterious, Solemn, Thoughtful, Nostalgic, Clinical, Detached, Philosophical, Measured, Serious.
items.negative: NEGATIVE: Oppressive, Melancholic, Foreboding, Bleak, Dismal, Hostile, Anxious, Isolated, Harrowing, Desolate.

items.supportive: SUPPORTIVE: Admiring, Affirming, Endorsing, Celebratory, Sympathetic, Encouraging, Empathetic, Respectful, Optimistic.
items.critical: CRITICAL: Condemning, Dismissive, Disparaging, Disapproving, Concerned, Sceptical, Alarmed, Cautionary, Cynical, Mocking, Critical.
items.balanced: BALANCED: Neutral, Objective, Measured, Analytical, Tentative, Contemplative, Evaluative, Inquisitive, Informative, Reserved, Considered.

synthesis.title: Synthesis sentence starters
items: Both texts show that…
While one writer believes…, the other…
A shared idea in both extracts is…
Text A highlights ____, whereas Text B emphasises ____.
Together, the texts suggest that…

shadesOfMeaning.title: Shades of meaning — choose your word carefully
items: Barren vs Desolate — barren is lifeless physical; desolate is emotional emptiness.
Endure vs Tolerate — endure implies strength through real hardship; tolerate is mild annoyance.
Relentless vs Determined — relentless is negative/aggressive; determined is positive willpower.

spag.title: SPaG essentials
items: It's = it is. Its = possession. Pronouns (yours, its, theirs) NEVER take an apostrophe.
Run-on sentences — fix with full stop, semicolon, or conjunction.
their / there / they're — possession / place / they are.
your / you're — possession / you are.
'Should of' is NEVER correct. Always 'should have' (could have, might have).
Apostrophes for possession: Luka's book (one Luka); the students' books (multiple students).

spelling.title: British vs American spelling
items: −our / −or: colour/color, honour/honor, behaviour/behavior, favour/favor, neighbour/neighbor.
−re / −er: centre/center, theatre/theater, metre/meter, litre/liter, calibre/caliber.
−ise / −ize: organise/organize, realise/realize, recognise/recognize, analyse/analyze.
Double consonants: travelling/traveling, labelled/labeled, cancelled/canceled, marvellous/marvelous.
Misc: aeroplane/airplane, maths/math, programme/program, grey/gray, cheque/check.
Use BRITISH spelling in IB MYP exams.

devicesExamples.title: Literary devices with worked examples
items: Personification — 'The wind whispered through the trees.'
Zoomorphism — describing humans as animals: 'She fought like a tiger.'
Repetition — emphasises emotion: 'I will not, I will not, I will not give up.'
Metaphor — 'Time is a thief.'
Alliteration — 'wild and windy weather.'
Hyperbole — deliberate exaggeration: 'I've told you a million times.'
Sensory imagery — appeals to five senses.
Simile — comparison with like/as: 'as cold as ice.'

extras.title: English — Extras
extras.intro: Topics that go deeper than the G9 revision list — critical thinking, classic literature, advanced vocabulary, exam-ready quotes.

criticalThinking.title: Critical thinking
items: Identify the claim — what is the author arguing?
Look for evidence — facts, statistics, expert opinion, anecdotes.
Spot fallacies — straw man, false dichotomy, ad hominem, appeal to authority.
Detect bias — emotional language, selective evidence, loaded questions.
Evaluate sources — peer-reviewed > newspaper > blog > social media.

shakespeare.title: Shakespeare — quick intro
shakespeare.body: William Shakespeare (1564–1616) wrote 39 plays and 154 sonnets. Key plays: Romeo and Juliet (tragedy), Macbeth (ambition), Hamlet (revenge, doubt), A Midsummer Night's Dream (comedy). Iambic pentameter — 10 syllables per line, alternating unstressed/stressed. Famous quote: 'To be, or not to be: that is the question.' Themes resonate today: love, power, identity, mortality.

vocab.title: Advanced vocabulary for essays
items: Ubiquitous — present everywhere (use instead of 'common').
Profound — having deep insight or impact.
Articulate (v) — express clearly; (adj) — well-spoken.
Paradox — apparent contradiction that reveals truth.
Catalyst — agent that causes change.
Pivotal — critically important.
Inevitable — certain to happen.
Furthermore / Moreover — and on top of that.
Notwithstanding — despite this.
Consequently / Therefore — as a result.

essayQuotes.title: Quotes to use in essays
items: On change: 'The only constant is change.' — Heraclitus
On knowledge: 'I think, therefore I am.' — Descartes
On society: 'It takes a village to raise a child.' — African proverb
On nature: 'In every walk with nature one receives far more than he seeks.' — John Muir
On power: 'With great power comes great responsibility.' — Voltaire (and Spider-Man)
On time: 'Tomorrow is the most important thing in life.' — John Wayne

poetry.title: Analysing poetry
poetry.body: 1) Read aloud at least twice. 2) Form: lines, stanzas, rhyme scheme (ABAB? AABB?). 3) Meter: count stressed syllables (iambic? trochaic?). 4) Imagery: sensory details, symbols. 5) Tone: melancholic, jubilant, ironic? 6) Theme: what big idea is explored? 7) Speaker: who is saying this and to whom? Pull short quotes when analysing — and always link back to the question.

textTypesPage.title: Text Types (Criterion C)
textTypesPage.intro: Nine text types you may be asked to write. Match the format and audience carefully.

essay.name: Essay
essay.description: Formal, structured argument. Introduction → thesis → body → conclusion.

letter.name: Letter
letter.description: Address, salutation, body, closing. Formality matches the recipient.

email.name: Email
email.description: Subject line, greeting, concise body, sign-off. More direct than a letter.

speech.name: Speech
speech.description: Greet the audience, hook, structured argument, call-to-action. Spoken cadence.

newspaper.name: Newspaper Article
newspaper.description: Headline, byline, lead paragraph (5W), inverted pyramid for details.

online.name: Online Article
online.description: Engaging title, short paragraphs, subheadings. Optional links/quotes.

blog.name: Blog Article
blog.description: Conversational tone, personal voice, first-person. Open ending invites comments.

guide.name: Revision Guide
guide.description: Clear headings, bullet points, definitions, examples — built for retention.

script.name: Script
script.description: Scene heading, character names, dialogue, stage directions in italics or brackets.

devicesPage.title: Literary Devices
devicesPage.intro: Identify devices when reading; use them deliberately when writing.

anaphora.name: Anaphora
anaphora.definition: Repetition of word(s) at the start of successive clauses.
anaphora.example: We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds…

personification.name: Personification
personification.definition: Giving human traits to non-human things.
personification.example: The wind whispered through the trees.

hyperbole.name: Hyperbole
hyperbole.example: I've told you a million times.

rhetoricalQuestion.name: Rhetorical question
rhetoricalQuestion.definition: A question asked for effect rather than for an answer.
rhetoricalQuestion.example: Who could resist such a deal?

onomatopoeia.name: Onomatopoeia
onomatopoeia.example: The bees buzzed; the door creaked.

irony.name: Irony
irony.definition: Saying one thing while meaning the opposite, or a situation that subverts expectation.
irony.example: A traffic warden's car gets a parking ticket.

symbolism.name: Symbolism
symbolism.definition: An object represents an abstract idea.

foreshadowing.name: Foreshadowing
foreshadowing.definition: Hint of what will happen later.
foreshadowing.example: Dark clouds gathered as the heroes set off.

grammarPage.title: Grammar Essentials
grammarPage.intro: Frequent grammar pitfalls and the rules that fix them.

items.present: Present simple — habits, facts: 'She writes essays.'
items.pastCont: Past continuous — ongoing past: 'She was writing.'
items.presentPerfect: Present perfect — past with present relevance: 'She has written three.'
items.future: Future — 'will' (decision) vs 'going to' (plan): 'I'll help' / 'I'm going to study.'

items.comma: Comma — pauses, lists, after introductory phrases.
items.semicolon: Semicolon (;) — joins two related independent clauses.
items.colon: Colon (:) — introduces a list, example, or explanation.
items.dash: Dash (—) — sudden break or emphasis. Em dash is longer than hyphen.

agreement.title: Subject–verb agreement
agreement.body: Singular subject → singular verb. 'The list of items is on the table' (list is). Plural subject → plural verb. Watch for words between subject and verb.

items.lessFewer: less (uncountable: less time) vs fewer (countable: fewer cars)
items.affectEffect: affect (verb: to influence) vs effect (noun: the result)

readingPage.title: Reading Skills
readingPage.intro: How to break down unseen texts in exam conditions.

items.skim: Skim — get the gist quickly (60 seconds)
items.scan: Scan — find specific information (names, dates, keywords)
items.annotate: Annotate — underline key phrases, note tone, mark devices
items.infer: Infer — read between the lines for meaning not directly stated

items.literal: Literal — 'What does the text say?' Find direct evidence.
items.inferential: Inferential — 'What does this suggest?' Combine clues + knowledge.
items.language: Language — analyse word choice, devices, structure. Quote + explain effect.
items.evaluation: Evaluation — 'How effective is X?' Take a position, support with evidence.

PEEL.title: PEEL paragraph structure
PEEL.body: Point — your claim. Evidence — quote from text. Explain — what does the quote show? Link — back to the question. Use PEEL for every analytical paragraph.

essaysPage.title: Essay Practice
essaysPage.intro: Sample essay prompts in the IB MYP style. Plan with a thesis, three points, and a conclusion.

essayPrompts.prompt: 'Social media does more harm than good.' Discuss.
essayPrompts.tips: Balance both sides. State your position clearly. Use real examples (Instagram, TikTok). Conclude decisively.

essayPrompts.prompt: 'School uniforms should be abolished.' Argue for or against.
essayPrompts.tips: Take one side firmly. Three reasons with evidence. Address counterargument and refute it.

essayPrompts.prompt: Write a story beginning with: 'The lights flickered, and then…'
essayPrompts.tips: Establish setting. Use varied sentence structure. Include sensory detail. End with a twist or resonance.

essayPrompts.prompt: Describe a place that is special to you. Bring it to life through the five senses.
essayPrompts.tips: Sight, sound, smell, touch, taste. Avoid clichés. Use figurative language sparingly.

essayPrompts.prompt: Write a speech persuading your school to take action on climate change.
essayPrompts.tips: Direct address ('We must…'). Rhetorical question. Tripartite list. Call to action at the end.

checklist: Match the text type and audience: formal letter ≠ blog post
Use a clear structure with paragraphs
Show, don't tell — use specific details
Proofread for spelling, punctuation, and tense consistency
